speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wise you were telling me that-- that you-- how did you get to the train? And how did you-- how did your body get off the-- the train in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean how? We walked to the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when you-- when you were in Dachau, and you got sick, and they put you-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember already. I've been like an a coma. You see, I-- they I hardly moved. You know. Only they see-- when somebody-- somebody moving, and they think, let's go. Maybe we try somebody to-- to bring alive. Only took three of them between thousands, what you see in the picture, thousands of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a matter of fact, about-- about five months ago, six months ago, we had a gathering. When I heard a quote from [INAUDIBLE], [? Are you ?] Mr. Wise? He read in paper the [INAUDIBLE]. I say, yes. My name is [INAUDIBLE], I am your liberator.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- my unit from an American liberate, you are the one who brought me in the hospital. When he came here, we took the pictures, what you see there. He got the pictures and everything. And-- and-- and he was one of the liberators. And I hear a lot, of course, from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell-- tell me about the day. How-- how did they find you? Who told you how they found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- their major, their major from the unit, he took every-- the pictures. And he took-- and he-- when he say, I am [? Ahid ?]. I am [? Agothid. ?] My mom is [? Agothid ?]. [NON-ENGLISH], don't kill me. Don't kill me. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] When he opened up his blouse and he took out the mezuzah. When I see the mezuzah, only he see the mezuzah, only he start to cry. And I start-- I been so emotional, and I start to cry. There's all the soldiers around start to cry. On every [? shoulder, ?] see, I'm-- I'm talking to somebody a little bit. Everybody who got chocolate, and cigarettes, and [INAUDIBLE] on bread. Everybody took-- [INAUDIBLE] it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the major said not to do nothing to him, not to talk to him. I be in a half hour. I'll be back. He came in the jeep with three doctors, American doctors-- the American-- German doctors. The American had them already prisoners of war. German doctors. When they brought him to my room, and you see-- you see the damn skeleton? If you can bring him alive, you be alive. If not, we going to do this to you what you did to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this doctor stayed to me around the clock. First of all, the doctor put a sheet, a lot of food on the floor of food. They say, [? take a bit-- ?] you don't have to [INAUDIBLE] at all. If not-- if not, he'd be dead in 10 minutes if he gonna taste it. Nothing. Take away from his eyes. He don't have to see this. Just stay around him clock, make drops, a drop of this, and a shot, and a drop. I don't know what they did to me anyway. But I came-- I came to me. And I see nurses. I take this as a dream or something. I didn't believe it. From the concentration camp, from [INAUDIBLE] life to people talking like-- like people to people. I-- I didn't believe I can't talk to people sometimes. Their major came to me every hour to see what their doing and how I'm doing. Well, it takes a little months, months of months till I came to me, till-- till I got-- I see what's going on out there, liberation, when I became [? zero ?] fit. This, I couldn't. My legs don't hold on the body. I crawl like a baby. I couldn't stand even. When the doctor say, this is all right. This is not fit. This is [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from what he didn't get, nothing in his body. Well, it took me almost about a year till I come back to my-- to my normal-- normal life. When the doctor say, if I be alive, if they can bring me alive, I could be blind, or deaf, or something like this. Something, you know, something got to be. What is-- what I lost is my smell. I lost my smell. When I think of all the [INAUDIBLE]. When I came here in United States, even the German government, even the American, the Federation, everybody send me to a doctor to-- to find out and say-- the doctors here in America say you're better off you don't smell it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, on a-- on a daily basis when you were in Dachau, what kept you going? Why-- how could you live every day? What kept you sane?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau? When we've been in Dachau, or we've been in ghetto, or we've been in the labor camps, we got only one thing in our mind. That's we don't believe we became alive. Because everybody know that it is not today. People, thousands and thousands have died every day there. People worked. Run around, you looked, you see, people there don't know whether you look like-- you don't see nothing at all. Because you see, today, there's your friend. Tomorrow you'll be there, same thing. Like in my barrack was a man with a son, a young son. The father passed away [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in the barracks. The son, the first thing what he did, he opened up his arm, only took out the piece of bread that he had for-- for next day to-- to eat. Because if not, if he wouldn't do that, the rest of them will do it. They will steal from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because [INAUDIBLE]? Because somebody couldn't stand from hunger, you know, from bread, you'd get up in the middle in the night, you know, and steal the piece of bread from some other guy, one from the other. This makes you, you know, a son to his father. He didn't-- he didn't care what he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: He know that someday-- but the piece of bread, [INAUDIBLE] It stayed me from my eyes now. And any time-- anytime I remember this-- [INAUDIBLE] there's already 50 years after liberation. But last year I still had-- had a dream, like they're going after me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found out I'm alive. And [INAUDIBLE] and they run after me. When I fall out from my-- from my bed, went on the night-- night table there. On the nightstand, I fall down. On-- on-- and I open up here. They make me 17 stitches here. When [INAUDIBLE] get up, and I say, Sam, what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: She sees the full bed with blood. [INAUDIBLE] she-- they didn't catch me. They didn't catch me. On my-- my-- son-in-law, he's a doctor. She call him right now. And he came right now. It was 3 o'clock in the morning. They took me right in hospital. They make me 17 stitches. And I still couldn't get over from-- from dreams already about 50 years after the liberation. I couldn't forget this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, every day, what did you think about every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't think. We didn't go on about that. That's all. Today and tomorrow, we did-- we'd be dead. Every day, we'd-- we never believe when this wasn't right. The ghettos, they found out-- in the ghettos are bunkers. You know bunkers? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] They came, the Nazis, right before the liberation. They found-- they came with dogs, with-- with ammunition, with-- with-- with everything to find out where Jewish people hiding there. And later on, they burned up all the houses, all the barracks and everything only not to found nobody alive. If somebody was lucky, the-- like my youngest brother one, the middle one, he was in the bunker when they burned them up. They found out he's there, you know. And they put a-- a bomb, something like this, you know, gas. And they kill a lot of people. But they got bunkers, you know. Some of them in the ghetto, they tried to make some-- let's say they make double walls. They took some children, little children, when they're putting in double walls. You know. But a child, you could not keep up not crying. People kill their own children before they going to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that your younger brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother-- got him. My brother there, what he hanged himself. He didn't go to the action, you know. He didn't come with my father and brother. But they killed him in ghetto. He was in a bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The middle one, Mottel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see them do this. But the rest of them got out alive. We know he's in ghetto. I know that he's in ghetto. But after the liberation, he not founded.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that you sang when--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] the last one, the little one. But they became 13 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you say that you-- didn't you sing some songs also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, I used to. I sang-- I sang because I was hungry. I sang. [LAUGHS] People listened to me. They became-- they forget about they are hungry, you know. They said to me, I am-- I am-- I am the doctor, what-- making everybody to be-- to stay alive. I tell them jokes, everything I can. I davened, we were-- I prayed for him, Yom Kippur. You know. Whenver-- it was Yom Kippur everyday there. But the same day for Yom Kippur is I used to-- I used daven for them, you know, the prayers, you know, Yom Kippur, prayers. And everybody after me, you know, we forgot about-- about the hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isn't there a particular song that you sang?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- any kind of songs, I sing. I'd make a-- a poem, like Yom Kippur [INAUDIBLE], Yom Kippur in this evening. Like everybody going to shul, only Germans, when the-- the Germans fighting with the [INAUDIBLE]. And all of a sudden the Germans coming in in shul, when they found out there's a gym, a-- a spy-- a spy is there hiding in shul. And they asking all the Jewish people to bring out the spy. If not, they burn up the shul. [? Many ?] people, I make them poem for this, you know. Everybody [INAUDIBLE], crying about them. This is a long long poem to tell this. But-- but I-- when the Germans, when the Germans say again, if in two minutes, if you don't give up the spy-- they're gonna burn-- there was already kerosene cans, with-- gasoline cans to burn up the synagogue with all the Jewish people. Nobody can find the spy there. But one man, 90 years old, he get up. And he say, I am this. I am the spy. He was 90 years blind. And he couldn't talk [INAUDIBLE]. I. I-- I am the spy. I am the spy. And I [? gone-- ?] I [? gone ?] for you, all of you. Let them kill me. I'm the spy. And they took him on a field. And they-- and they shoot him as a spy. When I say the last one is, nobody in the world-- God shows you people like that spy. He went for everybody. He saved everybody's life. It was all for his life. 90 years old. That's when everybody cried for him. In Yiddish-- you just-- I couldn't express this in English. But in Yiddish, it is a different poem. You know. When everybody cry, when everybody kiss me, when everybody hug me, you know, this is the reason. Maybe this-- this what keeps me going. This is the way I do it now too even in the Federation. Even we got Yiddish clubs now. I come in there. I make other people who lived then, [? on the crying, ?] and everything. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, this poem that you wrote, is that a true story about the 90-year-old man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I make them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You made up the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I make up especially for them. I make the story. Couldn't be-- the Germans are not any fools like this. A blind man of 90 years? I make our fool, the Germans are. This is-- this is my poem in this, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when--

speaker: interviewee
text: But because nobody-- nobody nation, nobody else can go offer himself to say I'm a spy only to save all the-- all the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you tell that poem? When did you say that poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And didn't you say that--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I say the [INAUDIBLE] on occasions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that song, [INAUDIBLE], did you sing that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. That's the old Yiddish song with all the world, all the Yiddish nation. No. It's the first song. There's a Haida song. Do you know what a Haida? A Haida song. A Haida song is always-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH] That's a part of them. The rest of them, I leave this for the next time. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you sing that song? From Haida, did you sing it while you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Sure, ?] I did. I sang all the traditional song, the Haida song. There's a Haida, like a rabbi sat down in a Haida when he learned the first ABC, [? alef-beis, ?] we called this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing it while you were in the ghetto and in Dachau too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. Yeah. I been hungry, I singing. I been tired, I singing. Or on-- on-- I couldn't write there in the ghetto. In the ghetto, I write about it in Dachau. If they found me writing something, I'd be already dead. [INAUDIBLE] didn't write. You didn't have to-- to have a pencil that [? you could use in Dachau. ?] Didn't have any paper to write in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: You lost there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, no. I'm fine. When you-- when you were in Dachau, though, were you ever punished in Dachau? Did they hurt you in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- I never been punished in Dachau. Especially punished, you know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any children in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Children in that camp? No children at all. The children-- the children with the old people, they made be killed right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'd mentioned. You said that-- when was the last time you saw children when you were-- during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the ghetto. Before they took the children, before they make the action for children. They call in another children action. They took all the children. They came in with big trucks in ghetto. On the-- they threw the children like they were throwing rocks with- with-- with records, you know, music, hard music, you know, not-- not to the people or the mothers, not-- not to hear how the children cried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody saw this, not me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I just want to ask you about the liberation again, if you don't mind. Do you remember being thrown off the train? Do you remember at all that you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I been in a coma. I've been in-- that's what-- You see, I didn't remember my liberation. I wish I would remember. Everybody-- everybody know about the liberation. The Army, the American Army came. They're kissing. And they're hugging each other. And-- and-- and they didn't know what to do. They dance in the streets. I didn't see this. I been-- I open-- I opened my-- never even seen American soldiers. You know, one time in ghetto, in-- in-- in Dachau, came from the Red Cross, came, some of them, they give every one of them a can of [INAUDIBLE], a [INAUDIBLE] of [INAUDIBLE]. My old friend, [INAUDIBLE] his meat, he got it in barrack. When he see the canned [INAUDIBLE], he hold the canned [INAUDIBLE] and he drink out. And he fall down like this, passed away from the [INAUDIBLE]. And later they say, who want to leave better not drink the [INAUDIBLE]. Like the Germans say, you see, the-- the American, make you-- they give you poison to drink, you see, the American-- the Germans, they like-- like the Red Cross give them poison. They help them to kill the-- the-- the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that year-- year was? Do you know which year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] There we be like animals. We didn't remember from-- from any dates, nothing at all. Some of them find out their names then. You see, didn't ever name him. Didn't ever call anybody by name. You got a number. That's [? what ?] forget their name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to remember. I don't want to remember. Better I don't remember. If I remember my number, the number will stay in front of my eyes every day. I don't want it. I'm glad I forgot my number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did you go after you were in the hospital? What happened after the hospital? Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been in Germany. I've been in Germany. I've been five years in Germany. I lived with my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you go to a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I my wife came, I been in the DP camp. Before I-- I-- I was a little stronger, you know, on my legs. But I-- I'd been like-- like a human being. I've been in Feldafing. There was a Feldafing camp. There's the-- the UNRRA took care of them. Only later on, when my wife came, I've been in Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German government give me apartment. They came in the-- the American government, the American Army gave me an apartment. They came in with me in a house. And they say, do you want? How many? You got your wife. OK? How many people do live here? To the Germans-- when there was SS people that used to live. He say, the American-- I remember it like today. He say, you got-- I give you three hours to move out because these people got to go in. They say, where we got to go? He say, you go to Dachau, in Auschwitz, and every village you want-- every camp you want to. It's free for you to go. But these people got to go-- [? have ?] apartments, yours. When you were in [? Federaline, ?] what-- how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In [? Fenderline, ?] the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Feldafing, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldafing. How long I been there? A short time, maybe about a half a year or eight months, something like this. I remember the camp, the last one that I've been, is my [INAUDIBLE] came here to me. This is the last time. And general-- I had pictures with General Eisenhower. But it got lost or somebody got it from me when I came over here. And I couldn't find it for nothing in the world. Then General Eisenhower came to camp to visit the-- the DP camp. If everything goes all right, is they put-- I remember like today. They put machine guns, you know, with soldiers around every corner in Feldafing. When General Eisenhower came like this form this area, I want to take away all the machine-- I haven't any enemies here in that camp. I don't want to see the machinery. I didn't want to see this. The [? machinery ?] is too much. When the soldiers took away the-- the-- when I came with a loaf of bread and with. Me and a lot-- a lot of them, that's a Jewish tradition. Then you go in a new house or you going in a new country, bread and salt, you know. When I broke this-- I brought it to him. And he-- I remember the speech, what they had. And you people-- America gonna be the first country in the world that they open up the gates for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Feldafing and went to-- where did you go next and what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I been in Munich. And I work for the-- I worked for in a-- you got my-- the-- what I give you the papers. I worked for a sport organization. [INAUDIBLE]. When I went the soccer game, you know, all kind of sport, when I been-- I work for them, the UNRRA kick me out. You know, they pay me for this too. And they give me food for this and everything. But I go on from every, like in Berlin, in Munich, in Stuttgart, in-- in Frankfurt, I arranged the DP-- from the DP camps who able to-- to make sport, you know, to soccer players, we make football-- soccer and all kinds of sport. I arranged this. I helped arrange people that would work with me together. And we went from one-- one-- from one town to the other town. And we arrived one Sunday or Saturday, we make Frankfurt play with Berlin, Berlin playing with Stuttgart. You know, and all-- all the camps, all the DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were in Munich, did-- didn't-- weren't you a writer?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish, I been a-- I got a press card. You got my press card still.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you write about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything what I see. You know, I seen DP camps. I see sometimes-- you know, I make a little poem what I write, you know. I see something DP-- in the DP camps, like somebody, what they do in there, how they-- how they trade in the [INAUDIBLE] of the house, somebody, you know, somebody buying this and somebody buying this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I make-- everything was for me interested. I write this. When I write this in poems, you know, to people to live, something like this-- when I write what happens-- in what happens-- [INAUDIBLE] especially in the UNRRA, how the UNRRA [INAUDIBLE]. How the UNRRA give them-- when I give a picture how we staying in a line before Passover, the UNRRA give herring. You know herring?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give herring for-- for Passover to have. Only I stand in a line with a picture. And I give this-- I give this to woman. She told me she going to give me the copy back. I-- she didn't ever do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you went to Munich. Did you ever think about going to Palestine after?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been. I've been married. My suitcase, my-- my boxes was ready to go to Israel, everything. But the last minute, my brother came in America eight months before me. We both, we still-- we both became alive from our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When my cousin-- he's now in Canada. He came to me. When he told me, Sam, you got a brother. But he's in America. And you go to Israel, why you got to separate together? Go first to America. From America to Israel, you can go anytime you want to. Stay. Be the family together at least.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came, I came in-- in-- I came in here in Atlanta. Only-- only really in Atlanta, I didn't-- I-- everybody got a contract, you know, like some are rich man, what they got a [INAUDIBLE] or something. What-- what he give you a-- a affidavit. Like the government don't have responsibility for me to keep me out. He gonna take care of me. There was one man from a [? garment ?] factory. A weaver. I became a weaver. You know. On-- on-- he took-- he wrote down. [INAUDIBLE] This was in Chattanooga, Tennessee. Only Chattanooga, Tennessee, I put on my backs, I marked out Israel. I put Chattanooga, Tennessee. I never been.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I never know what Chattanooga is. Only in-- in Germans or in any kind of language, they don't spell Chattanooga like here. Like they're the written-- they ask me. When I came here in New York, he said, where are you going? I say, 'Hat-a-noocha.' And everybody start to say, they say-- the man say, 'Hat-a-noocha,' I-- I think you got a mistake. He say, the United States don't have a country like this. [LAUGHS] Another state like this? United States-- Chattanooga. When I spell this, I read 'Hat-a-noocha.' And everybody laughed. I got a poem about them too. Do you remember that poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I got so many poems. But to write down, every occasion sometimes. We got birthdays. We got parties. When we got gatherings and everything, I write poems. And I-- and I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you married your wife. Where did you marry her? And when did you marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, when she came to me. It was in 19 something and [? probably ?] something. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you-- when you say that you did the Chattanooga destination. How-- how is it that you came over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it Chattanooga? How did you come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't been in Chattanooga. I-- I came-- I came here in the United State in New York. Through New York, I got to go to Atlanta. But the Federation from New York met me there. And they give me, you know, they give me the-- the instructions, made the papers, made the tickets to go to Atlanta. And they give me $17 in dollar bills to buy for the-- for the-- some food, underwear-- which I got [INAUDIBLE] was three years old-- to-- to buy for milk or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I got the $17 in my pocket. I never-- I never-- because I don't know how to-- how to spell milk, how to ask milk, and how to food, it was better I didn't buy nothing at all. But I came in Atlanta. I came in Atlanta. The Federation in Atlanta picked me up too.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here, and they got to make up the papers in Federation. When they asked me questions, my name, if there are children. They asked me everything. One a-- one secretary, [INAUDIBLE] she writing for me everything. [? She ?] [? say, ?] how many-- you got children? I say, yes. She said, how many children you got? I said, 17. Everybody start to laugh. And she fall down like this laughing on the chair. On the-- the-- the president from Federation was calm. He-- and he speak in perfect Yiddish. Only he hear everybody laughing here. What going on? What you laugh in here. They say, Mr. [? Kahn ?], that man came just down from Europe. But he left 17 children in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me is Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. I say, no. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] what the Federation give me. And I put down the $17. Well, I put on the table. Well, everybody start to laugh. And the-- You see, this-- this means a lot when you don't know nothing. I thought she asked me how much money I got. I say, yeah. I got 17. There she is. She's saying I got 17 children in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect-- I know you had Saba. How many children?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god. I'm sorry. Well, that's good. Perfect, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect timing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna be on the last. How many takes did we use? Three, right? Fourth one coming up. You know, I want to see if there's--
